entries.notes: alsoKnownAs: Scarlet Skunk Cleaner Shrimp, Pacific Cleaner Shrimp, Ambon Cleaner Shrimp, Whitebanded Cleaner Shrimp. Foundational reef cleaner shrimp; sets up cleaning stations and removes parasites/dead tissue from fish. Reef safe with caution (may pick at LPS occasionally). Hermaphroditic — any two will pair.

alsoKnownAs: Fire Shrimp, Blood Shrimp, Red Cleaner Shrimp, Scarlet Fire Shrimp. Brilliant red Lysmata cleaner; more reclusive than L. amboinensis. Reef safe. Same hermaphroditic pairing as amboinensis.

alsoKnownAs: Caribbean Peppermint, Aiptasia-Eating Shrimp. Famous for eating Aiptasia anemone pests. Taxonomic note: 'Peppermint Shrimp' is actually a complex of several species (L. wurdemanni, L. boggessi, L. ankeri, L. rathbunae); only some are reliable Aiptasia eaters. L. boggessi is the Florida species typically sold as 'Peppermint'. Research agent should flag the complex.
alsoKnownAs: Atlantic Cleaner Shrimp, Scarlet Lady Cleaner, Mountain Cleaner Shrimp. Caribbean/Atlantic counterpart to L. amboinensis with similar care and behavior; slightly less common in trade.

alsoKnownAs: Kuekenthal's Shrimp, Indo-Pacific Cleaner Shrimp. Rare Indo-Pacific Lysmata occasionally available through specialty importers. Care parallels other Lysmata cleaners. Specialty entry — sources may be thin.

alsoKnownAs: Anemone Shrimp, High-Tail Shrimp, Dancing Shrimp (some sources). Tiny (~1-2 cm) social nano shrimp that lives commensally with anemones; named for the characteristic wiggling tail-up posture. Best in groups of 5+. Reef safe.

alsoKnownAs: Periclimenes pedersoni (older genus placement), Caribbean Anemone Shrimp, Translucent Cleaner Shrimp. Caribbean anemone-symbiont cleaner shrimp. Reef safe. Often kept with Condylactis or corkscrew anemones as host.

alsoKnownAs: Pacific Cleaner Shrimp, Anemone Partner Shrimp, White-spotted Anemone Shrimp. Indo-Pacific anemone-commensal cleaner — typically with Heteractis or Stichodactyla carpet anemones. Distinct from the Caribbean Spotted Anemone Shrimp (Periclimenes yucatanicus) — same common-name family, different species and host range. Reef safe.

alsoKnownAs: Yucatan Anemone Shrimp, Caribbean Spotted Anemone Shrimp, Caribbean Spotted Cleaner. Caribbean/Atlantic anemone-commensal cleaner — typically with Condylactis gigantea (Atlantic giant anemone) or corkscrew anemones. Distinct from the Indo-Pacific Spotted Cleaner Shrimp (Periclimenes brevicarpalis) — different species, different host anemones, different ocean. Reef safe. Aquarium Breeder has a dedicated species guide.

alsoKnownAs: Painted Harlequin Shrimp, Clown Shrimp. Obligate predator of starfish — eats live sea stars (Asterias, Linckia, etc.). Specialist species, often kept as a pair to control pest stars. Reef safe to corals but devastates the cleanup crew if it includes sea stars. Often kept in dedicated nano species tanks. (FW common-name collision with Caridina spp. — different family entirely.)

alsoKnownAs: Banded Coral Shrimp, Banded Boxer Shrimp, Boxer Shrimp (not to be confused with Lybia tessellata pom-pom crab). Striking red-and-white cleaner shrimp. Pugnacious toward conspecifics and other shrimp — best kept as a true pair (bought small together) or solo. Reef safe but predatory toward small ornamental shrimp and small fish.

alsoKnownAs: Striped Bumblebee Shrimp. Tiny (~1.5 cm) yellow-and-black banded shrimp that feeds primarily on echinoderm tube-feet. Reef safe to corals but predatory toward sea stars and urchins. (FW common-name collision with Caridina.)

alsoKnownAs: Common Marble Shrimp, Saron Shrimp, Buffalo Shrimp. Crepuscular/nocturnal Indo-Pacific shrimp with distinctive bristly setae on the carapace. Reef safe to corals but will pick at zoanthids and tubeworms. Predatory toward smaller invertebrates.

alsoKnownAs: Dancing Shrimp, Hingebeak Shrimp, Candy Shrimp. Inexpensive striped red-and-white shrimp often sold as a starter ornamental. NOT fully reef safe — will pick at zoanthids, soft corals, and LPS polyps. Flag this caveat prominently in research.

alsoKnownAs: Striped Pistol Shrimp, Snapping Shrimp, Goby Partner Shrimp (most common shrimp-goby symbiont in the trade). Forms famous symbiotic relationships with watchman gobies (Cryptocentrus, Stonogobiops). Stuns prey with cavitation-bubble snap from its enlarged claw. Reef safe.
alsoKnownAs: Candy Cane Pistol Shrimp, Red-Banded Pistol Shrimp. Smaller striped Alpheus, often paired with Stonogobiops yasha (Yasha goby) or other small partner gobies. Reef safe.

alsoKnownAs: Striped Pistol Shrimp (generic). Trade name for an unspecified Alpheus species — research agent should attempt to identify by morphology against known Alpheus species (A. soror, A. ochrostriatus candidates) or accept genus-level identification. Reef safe. Tradeoff: real species identity is uncertain.

alsoKnownAs: Harlequin Mantis Shrimp, Painted Mantis, Clown Mantis. Famous 'smasher' mantis with hypersonic dactyl strike capable of cavitation and tank-glass damage. Species-only tank required (acrylic recommended over glass). Reef safe to corals but destroys all tank-mates. ~15-18 cm. Highly intelligent, often becomes the centerpiece of a dedicated mantis setup.

alsoKnownAs: Ternate Mantis, Small Smasher Mantis. Compact (~5-8 cm) smasher mantis — same general care as O. scyllarus but lower tank-volume requirement and dactyl-strike damage potential. Species-only tank still strongly recommended.

alsoKnownAs: Debelius Reef Lobster, Pink Reef Lobster, Purple Spotted Lobster, Indigo Reef Lobster. Nocturnal Indo-Pacific reef lobster with vivid purple and orange coloration. Reef safe with caution (may grab fish at night). Reclusive — needs caves.
alsoKnownAs: Hawaiian Reef Lobster, Pacific Reef Lobster, Red Pygmy Reef Lobster. Smaller cousin to E. debelius, similar care. Nocturnal, reclusive, reef-safe-with-caution.

alsoKnownAs: Equal-Handed Hermit, Dwarf Blue Leg Hermit, Caribbean Blue Leg Hermit. Workhorse Caribbean cleanup-crew hermit. Small (~1-2 cm), peaceful, algae and detritus consumer. Provide spare shells of multiple sizes to prevent shell-fighting.

alsoKnownAs: Red Reef Hermit, Red Tip Hermit, Cadenat's Hermit, Mexican Red Leg Hermit (in some trade). Standard Caribbean cleanup-crew hermit. Slightly larger and more aggressive than Clibanarius tricolor; eats algae, cyanobacteria, detritus. Reef safe.

alsoKnownAs: Striped Hermit, Orange and Black Hermit, Hawaiian Orange Hermit. Indo-Pacific ornamental hermit with vivid orange/red and black-striped legs. Larger than the Caribbean cleanup hermits (~5-7 cm); more of a display species than a workhorse cleaner. Reef safe but may topple frags.

alsoKnownAs: Electric Orange Hermit, Blue-Knuckle Hermit, Striped Hermit. Indo-Pacific ornamental hermit with electric blue legs and black-and-white striped antennae. Mid-sized cleanup helper. Reef safe.

alsoKnownAs: Red Leg Hermit, Mexican Hermit, Baja Red-Leg Hermit. Mid-sized Eastern Pacific (Gulf of California) hermit with bright red walking legs. Common in cleanup-crew packages — sometimes confused with Paguristes cadenati (Caribbean Red Reef Hermit); the two are sold under similar trade names but are distinct species from different oceans.

alsoKnownAs: Anemone Hermit Crab, Tube Anemone Hermit, Dardanus Hermit. Large Indo-Pacific hermit famous for actively transferring sea anemones (Calliactis) onto its shell as defensive partners. Reef safe but tank-mate-aggressive at adult size.

alsoKnownAs: Mithrax Crab, Green Mithrax, Bubble Algae Crab. Famous bubble-algae (Valonia) eater; standard reef cleanup-crew member. Mostly reef safe but adult specimens may opportunistically grab small fish or pick at LPS — flag the size-related risk.

alsoKnownAs: Spotted Porcelain Crab, Anemone Crab, White-Spotted Porcelain Crab. Filter-feeding anemone commensal (typically with Heteractis or Stichodactyla carpet anemones). Reef safe. Taxonomy note: traded as both N. maculatus and N. ohshimai — Aqua Imports labels theirs N. ohshimai. Research agent should record both attributions.

alsoKnownAs: Boxer Crab, Pompom Crab, Lybia Boxer Crab. Tiny (~2 cm) crab that carries living anemones (typically Triactis or Bunodeopsis) in its claws as defensive 'pom poms'. Reef safe. (SW common-name collision with the FW Pom Pom Crab Ptychognathus barbatus — different family entirely.)

alsoKnownAs: Yellowline Arrow Crab, Spider Crab (trade — distinct from true spider crabs), Caribbean Arrow Crab. Distinctive elongated body with triangular carapace and very long legs. Reef safe to corals but opportunistic predator of bristleworms, small ornamental shrimp, and very small fish.

alsoKnownAs: Spider Decorator Crab, Velcro Crab. Famously attaches sponges, algae, and even small corals to its carapace as camouflage. Reef safe to corals broadly but may detach frags to wear them — flag the frag-theft caveat.

alsoKnownAs: Spray Crab, Nimble Spray Crab, Reef Spider Crab. Fast Caribbean/Atlantic algae-grazer often added for cleanup. Reef safe at small size but adults become opportunistic and may grab small fish — flag the size-related risk.

alsoKnownAs: Harlequin Crab, Sea Cucumber Crab, Commensal Crab. Niche commensal crab that lives among sea anemones or sea cucumbers. Reef safe. (Common-name collision with the symbiont Porcelain Anemone Crab Neopetrolisthes — different family, distinct biology.)
alsoKnownAs: Mithrax Clinging Crab, Red Mithrax. Smaller congener of the Emerald Crab; same bubble-algae-eating habit, less common in trade. Reef safe.

alsoKnownAs: Tigger Pods (brand trade name), Splash-Pool Copepod, California Tigriopus, Red Sea Pod. The standard reef-tank live food + cleanup copepod. Marine, harpacticoid. Cultured commercially for picky fish (mandarins, pipefish, leopard wrasses). Entry frames husbandry from the culture-keeper / refugium-stocking perspective.

alsoKnownAs: Tisbe Copepod, Reef Cleanup Pod, Detritus Pod. Smaller, benthic-loving cleanup copepod; sometimes paired with Tigriopus in commercial cultures. Cultured for refugia and to feed picky fish. Marine, harpacticoid. Reef tank stalwart for biological cleanup.

alsoKnownAs: Apocyclops Copepod, Pelagic Copepod, Brackish Copepod. Pelagic (water-column) cyclopoid copepod commercially cultured as an alternative/complement to Tigriopus for water-column-feeding fish (e.g., wild seahorses, anthias fry). Cultured form is brackish-to-marine. Smaller than Tigriopus; preferred for very small fry.

alsoKnownAs: Americamysis bahia (modern genus), Opossum Shrimp, Live Mysid, Saltwater Mysid. Not a true shrimp — Mysida order. Cultured live food for picky reef fish (seahorses, pipefish, mandarins, fry of pelagic spawners). Brackish-to-marine. Entry frames husbandry from the culture-keeper perspective.

alsoKnownAs: Common Saron, Bristly Saron, Green Saron. Sister species to S. marmoratus; similar nocturnal/predatory behavior. Less common in trade. Research agent may need to fold under S. marmoratus if sources are too thin.

alsoKnownAs: Gonodactylus Mantis, Spotted Smasher, Common Smashing Mantis. Smaller smasher mantis (~7-10 cm), more readily kept in reasonable-sized acrylic tanks than O. scyllarus. Genus-level umbrella entry — trade is often genus-only (G. chiragra, G. smithii, etc.). Species-only tank still required.
